section.title: Those Who Sow in Tears
section.sectionRationale: A Song of Ascents, seventh in the pilgrim collection Psalms 120 to 134, placed in Book V of the Psalter, Psalms 107 to 150. The psalm moves from remembered, dream-like joy at a past restoration to an honest prayer for a restoration still incomplete, and closes with a promise that present tears are not wasted.
section.thesis: The LORD's past restoration of His people, remembered like a dream, fuels present prayer for further restoring grace and confident hope that those who sow in tears will reap with shouts of joy, a pattern fulfilled in Christ's own path from weeping to resurrection joy.
section.frameNote: Three movements: joyful remembrance of a restoration already accomplished, verses 1 to 3, a prayer for continued restoration, verse 4, and the closing promise that sowing in tears leads to reaping in joy, verses 5 to 6.

verses.text: When the LORD restored the captives of Zion, we were like dreamers.
Then our mouths were filled with laughter, our tongues with {{rinnah|shouts of joy}}. Then it was said among the nations, "The LORD has done great things for them."
The LORD has done great things for us; we are filled with joy.
Restore our captives, O LORD, like streams in the Negev.
Those who sow in tears will reap with {{rinnah|shouts of joy}}.
He who goes out weeping, bearing a trail of seed, will surely return with shouts of joy, carrying sheaves of grain.

rinnah.gloss: A ringing cry or shout, whether of joy or of lament; here the exuberant shout that erupts at deliverance, verse 2, and the shout promised to greet the harvest after tears, verses 5 to 6.

basic.context: This Song of Ascents likely looks back on the return from Babylonian exile as a dream-like deliverance, then honestly admits the restoration is not yet complete, praying for it to continue like sudden streams filling the dry Negev, and promising that present sorrow will end in joy.
basic.hermeneutics: It describes a specific, remembered national restoration and the ongoing need for further restoring grace, and it prescribes as an abiding pattern remembering God's past deliverances while praying honestly for present need and trusting His promised harvest of joy.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm turns on rinnah, a ringing shout of joy, heard first at the LORD's great deliverance and promised again at the harvest that follows tearful sowing, binding past rescue and future hope together in one word.
basic.typology: The pattern of sowing in tears and reaping in joy anticipates Christ's own path from the sorrow of the cross to the joy of resurrection, and it anticipates the pattern of costly gospel labor that yields an eternal harvest.
basic.christ: Jesus describes His own death using the language of a seed sown, John 12:24, going out in weeping to the cross and returning with the shout of resurrection joy, and He secures the pattern this psalm promises, that sorrow sown in faith is never wasted.
basic.god: The LORD is the one who restores His people from captivity, so unmistakably that even the surrounding nations recognize His great deeds, and He is the one who can flood dry, barren places, like the seasonal streams of the Negev, with sudden abundance and life.
basic.crossReferences: John 12:24 pictures Christ as a grain of wheat that falls, dies, and bears much fruit, the deepest fulfillment of tearful sowing. Galatians 6:9 promises a harvest in due season to those who do not grow weary. Revelation 21:4 promises the final wiping away of every tear.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's movement from remembered deliverance to honest prayer to promised harvest is fulfilled in Christ, who sowed His own life in tears at the cross and reaped the joy of resurrection, guaranteeing that His people's tearful labor will likewise end in joy.

context.literary: A Song of Ascents that moves from past-tense celebration, verses 1 to 3, to present-tense petition, verse 4, to a future-tense promise cast in proverbial agricultural imagery, verses 5 to 6.
context.historical: Most naturally read against the return of exiles from Babylon under Cyrus's decree, a genuine but incomplete restoration, since Jerusalem's walls and full national life still needed rebuilding, explaining the honest prayer of verse 4.
context.canonical: Follows Psalm 125's confidence in the LORD's ongoing protection with the memory of His decisive past restoration, and anticipates the New Testament's pattern of a costly, tearful sowing that yields a certain, joyful harvest.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To root present hope in a remembered, unmistakable act of the LORD's restoring grace, to teach honest prayer for further restoration where it is still needed, and to promise that tearful, faithful labor will end in a joyful harvest.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific national restoration, likely the return from exile, still incomplete at the time of writing. It prescribes as an abiding pattern remembering God's past deliverances, praying honestly amid ongoing need, and trusting His promise that sown tears yield a reaped joy.

debates.question: Are the tears of verses 5 to 6 the grief of exile itself, or the ordinary hardship of faithful labor and mission?

views.label: The specific grief of exile and return
views.case: The psalm's immediate context is Zion's captivity and restoration, so the tears most naturally picture the sorrow of exile giving way to the joy of homecoming.

views.label: A general principle of costly, faithful labor
views.case: The proverbial, agricultural form of verses 5 to 6, detached from any named event, suggests a broader principle applicable to any faithful sowing done through hardship.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that God turns tearful, faithful labor into a certain and joyful harvest, whether that labor is corporate restoration or ongoing gospel work.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm is bound together by rinnah, a ringing shout of joy, first heard at the LORD's unmistakable deliverance, verse 2, and promised again at the harvest that follows tearful sowing, verses 5 to 6, so that past rescue becomes the pattern for future hope.

typology.type: Restoration so evident even the nations recognize it, verses 1 to 2
typology.fulfillment: Christ's resurrection, a deliverance so decisive that even hostile powers must reckon with it, Acts 2:32-36.

typology.type: Sowing in tears, reaping in joy, verses 5 to 6
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the grain of wheat that falls into the earth and dies, bearing much fruit, John 12:24, and the pattern of all costly, faithful gospel labor, Galatians 6:9.

study.christ: Jesus describes His own coming death in this psalm's very language, a grain of wheat that must fall into the earth and die in order to bear much fruit, John 12:24, going out in the weeping of Gethsemane and the cross and returning with the shout of resurrection joy. He secures forever the pattern this psalm promises, that tears sown in faithfulness to God are never wasted but yield a certain and abundant harvest, Galatians 6:9.
study.god: The LORD is the one who restores His people from captivity so unmistakably that even surrounding nations recognize His great deeds, and He is the one who can flood dry, barren ground, like the seasonal streams of the Negev, with sudden and abundant life, both now and in answer to ongoing prayer.

crossReferences.note: Christ as the grain of wheat that falls and dies to bear much fruit, the deepest fulfillment of tearful sowing.
Promises a harvest in due season to those who do not grow weary in doing good, echoing this psalm's promised reaping.
The final wiping away of every tear, the ultimate horizon toward which this psalm's promised joy points.

study.oneStory: This psalm's movement from remembered deliverance to honest prayer to promised harvest is fulfilled in Christ, who sowed His own life in tears at the cross and reaped the joy of the resurrection, guaranteeing that His people's tearful, faithful labor will likewise end in joy.

sermon.title: Praying Our Tears
sermon.why: Keller expounds Psalm 126 directly, teaching believers to expect tears, invest tears, and pray their tears in light of this psalm's promise that sowing in tears leads to a harvest of joy.

quiz.q: How does the psalm describe the people's reaction to the LORD's restoration of Zion's captives in verses 1 to 2?

options.text: Like dreamers, with mouths filled with laughter and shouts of joy.
options.note: This dream-like, joyful astonishment is the exact response the psalm describes.

options.text: With cautious suspicion that it might not be real.
options.note: The psalm's tone is unrestrained joy, not suspicion.

options.text: With silence and no visible reaction.
options.note: The text describes overflowing laughter and shouts of joy, not silence.

options.text: With immediate plans for military conquest.
options.note: The response described is joyful praise, not a call to war.

quiz.q: What did the nations say about the LORD's work for His people, according to verse 2?

options.text: "The LORD has done great things for them."
options.note: This is the exact declaration the psalm attributes to the surrounding nations.

options.text: "This nation was fortunate by chance."
options.note: The nations explicitly credit the LORD, not chance.

options.text: "Their own army achieved this victory."
options.note: The credit given is to the LORD, not to human military strength.

options.text: "They should be feared and avoided."
options.note: The nations' declaration is recognition of the LORD's deeds, not a statement of fear or avoidance.

quiz.q: What image does verse 4 use to picture the prayer for continued restoration?

options.text: Streams suddenly filling the dry Negev.
options.note: This is the specific image of sudden, life-giving abundance the psalm uses.

options.text: A slow trickle that never fully arrives.
options.note: The image is of sudden, abundant flooding, not a slow trickle.

options.text: A well that has run permanently dry.
options.note: The prayer expects reversal and abundance, not permanent dryness.

options.text: Rain falling gently over many months.
options.note: The Negev streams image pictures sudden, dramatic renewal, not gradual gentle rain.

quiz.q: According to verses 5 to 6, what is promised to those who sow in tears?

options.text: They will reap with shouts of joy, returning with sheaves of grain.
options.note: This is the psalm's exact promise, tearful sowing ending in joyful harvest.

options.text: They will never see any harvest at all.
options.note: The psalm promises a certain, joyful harvest, not its absence.

options.text: They will reap exactly as much sorrow as they sowed.
options.note: The promise is a reversal, joy replacing tears, not equal sorrow returned.

options.text: They will need to sow again immediately with no rest.
options.note: The image is of a completed, joyful return with sheaves, not endless unrelieved labor.

quiz.q: How does Jesus draw on this psalm's pattern of sowing and reaping?

options.text: He describes His own death as a grain of wheat that falls and dies to bear much fruit.
options.note: John 12:24 directly applies this sowing-and-reaping pattern to Christ's death and resurrection.

options.text: He rejects any connection between His death and agricultural imagery.
options.note: Jesus explicitly uses this very imagery of a seed dying to bear fruit.

options.text: He applies it only to future generations, never to Himself.
options.note: Jesus applies the image directly to His own coming death and its fruit.

options.text: He uses it to describe only the disciples' future business ventures.
options.note: The imagery in John 12:24 is about Christ's own death and the fruit it bears, not commerce.

questions: The psalm remembers a past restoration like a dream, joy that felt almost too good to be true. What has God done for you that still feels that way when you recall it?
Even though the restoration was real, the psalmist still prays for it to continue, like streams filling a dry desert. What incomplete restoration are you still praying for God to finish in your life?
The psalm promises that those who sow in tears will reap with shouts of joy. What tearful season of faithfulness are you currently in, and how does this promise encourage you to keep sowing?
Jesus applied this very pattern of tearful sowing and joyful reaping to His own death and resurrection. How does His example shape the way you view your own costly obedience to Him?
The nations recognized that 'the LORD has done great things' for His people. How might your own story of God's restoring grace serve as a testimony to those watching your life?

translationNotes.note: Restored the captives of Zion can also be rendered restored the fortunes of Zion, a broader phrase covering any reversal from distress to well-being, not only literal captivity.
Streams in the Negev refers to seasonal wadis, dry riverbeds that flood suddenly and dramatically after rain, a vivid image for sudden, abundant restoration.
Bearing a trail of seed pictures a sower scattering precious seed grain during a hard season, so that its eventual harvest is all the more a cause for joy.

meta.notes: BSB text taken verbatim from biblehub.com's BSB page for Psalm 126 (the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error, so the web fallback was used for all of Psalms 124-132). Verse count (6) and wording cross-checked against the section map bounds Psalms 126:1-6. Footnote markers [a][b][c] present on biblehub for verses 1 and 4 were stripped per instructions; no em or en dashes present. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Tim Keller's sermon 'Praying Our Tears' (on Psalm 126) at gospelinlife.com.
